text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 59-year-old female with history of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head/uncinate process, status post neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles), consolidative chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy with concurrent capecitabine), and pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with pathologic ypT2 ypN2 M0 disease. Subsequently developed biopsy-confirmed hepatic metastases during adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine chemotherapy. Currently receiving second-line nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI)/leucovorin/5-FU (NAPOLI-1 regimen), now after 4 cycles. Restaging to evaluate treatment response. CA 19-9 was 58 U/mL at initiation of second-line therapy.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast at a rate of 3 mL/sec using a portal venous phase acquisition protocol. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were generated. Comparison made to prior CT abdomen/pelvis obtained approximately 8 weeks earlier at the time of hepatic metastasis diagnosis, and to prior CT obtained approximately 4 months before that at the midpoint restaging during adjuvant GemCap.


FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, ground-glass opacities, or consolidation. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.
Mediastinum: The mediastinal contour is within normal limits. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta demonstrates normal caliber without aneurysmal dilatation.
Chest wall and bones: No suspicious osseous lesions within the visualized thoracic skeleton. No rib lesions.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is slightly enlarged in craniocaudal dimension compared to the prior examination. There are now four hepatic lesions identified, all in the right hepatic lobe, representing an increase in both size and number since the prior study.

- Segment VI lesion: Previously measured 9 mm on CT at the time of the initial hepatic metastasis diagnosis; now measures approximately 2.1 cm in greatest dimension. The lesion demonstrates peripheral/rim arterial enhancement with central hypoenhancement on portal venous phase, consistent with a hypovascular metastatic deposit.

- Segment VII lesion: Previously measured 7 mm; now measures approximately 1.6 cm. Similar enhancement pattern with peripheral hypoenhancing core, morphologically consistent with metastatic disease.

- Segment IV lesion: This is newly identified compared to all prior imaging. It measures approximately 1.2 cm. Located in the medial segment of the left lobe adjacent to the gallbladder fossa. Demonstrates hypoenhancing character on portal venous phase with a subtle rim of arterial enhancement. No prior imaging correlate.

- Segment VIII lesion: Also newly identified compared to all prior studies. Measures approximately 0.8 cm. Hypoenhancing on portal venous phase. Given the clinical context and pattern, this is morphologically consistent with a metastatic deposit, though its small size limits full characterization.

No hepatic lesions are identified in segments I, II, III, or V beyond the above. No evidence of hepatic parenchymal replacement. No biliary ductal dilatation. No hepatic abscess or biloma. The common hepatic duct and intrahepatic bile ducts are decompressed and not dilated, consistent with the prior biliary stent history and post-Whipple anatomy.

Gallbladder: Absent, consistent with prior cholecystectomy performed as part of the Whipple procedure.

Spleen: Mildly enlarged at 13.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension (previously approximately 11.8 cm). This may reflect portal hypertensive changes in the setting of progressive hepatic metastatic burden, though no discrete splenic lesions are identified. No perisplenic fluid.

Pancreas: The pancreatic remnant (body and tail) is present, partially atrophic, consistent with prior pancreaticoduodenectomy. No discrete mass or hypodense lesion is identified in the pancreatic body or tail to suggest locoregional recurrence in the pancreatic remnant. The main pancreatic duct in the body/tail is mildly dilated at approximately 4 mm, stable to minimally changed, likely reflecting post-surgical/radiation changes and parenchymal atrophy.

Surgical bed and locoregional disease: Surgical clips are again present in the right upper quadrant and hepatoduodenal ligament region, consistent with known operative history. There is mature post-surgical fibrosis and desmoplastic change in the pancreaticoduodenectomy bed. No discrete soft tissue mass or nodularity is identified in the surgical bed to suggest locoregional recurrence at the Whipple anastomotic sites or the SMA/SMV region. The hepaticojejunostomy and pancreaticojejunostomy regions appear stable in appearance with no evidence of anastomotic leak or fluid collection.

Adrenal glands: The bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhancement. No hydronephrosis, nephrolithiasis, or discrete renal lesions.

Stomach and small bowel: The stomach is decompressed. The duodenojejunostomy anastomosis is seen in the right upper quadrant with no obstruction. The small bowel loops demonstrate no evidence of obstruction or wall thickening. No pneumatosis.

Colon: The colon appears unremarkable. No colonic wall thickening, mass, or obstructive process identified.

Peritoneum and ascites: A small-to-moderate volume of free fluid is present in the abdomen, layering in the pelvis and along the right paracolic gutter. This is new compared to the prior study. The ascites appears low-density without internal complexity to suggest abscess; however, in this clinical context, malignant ascites cannot be excluded and is considered likely given the degree of hepatic metastatic involvement. No discrete peritoneal nodules or omental caking are identified on this examination, though peritoneal metastases at this early stage can be below the resolution of CT.

Vascular structures: The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and portal venous system are patent. The portal vein is patent without evidence of thrombosis. The hepatic veins are patent. No superior mesenteric vein thrombosis or occlusion. Mild periportal edema is present, which in the context of hepatic metastatic disease likely reflects hepatic parenchymal stress.

Lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or pelvic lymph nodes are identified. No para-aortic lymphadenopathy. The previously noted peripancreatic nodes in the surgical bed are not enlarged.

Bones: The visualized skeleton demonstrates no lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastatic disease. No vertebral body collapse or fracture.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder is partially filled with no wall thickening or filling defects. The uterus and adnexal structures are not well visualized given streak artifact and position; no discrete pelvic masses are identified. No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid beyond what is described above.


IMPRESSION:

1. Hepatic metastatic disease - worsening. The four hepatic lesions now identified represent a marked increase in both size and number compared to the prior examination obtained approximately 8 weeks ago. The two previously known right hepatic lesions (segments VI and VII) have more than doubled in size, and two additional new lesions are now identified in segments IV and VIII. The overall hepatic metastatic burden is substantially worse compared to the time of initiation of second-line nal-IRI-based therapy.

2. New small-to-moderate volume ascites, most likely malignant in etiology given the degree of hepatic metastatic burden; no discrete peritoneal implants identified on this study, though this does not exclude peritoneal involvement.

3. No evidence of locoregional recurrence in the pancreaticoduodenectomy bed.

4. No pulmonary metastases or pleural disease.

5. No osseous metastatic disease within the imaged skeleton.

6. Mild splenic enlargement, likely reflecting portal hypertensive physiology in the setting of advancing hepatic disease.

Serum CA 19-9 at the time of this scan: 312 U/mL, representing a marked rise from 58 U/mL at initiation of nal-IRI/5-FU/LV, concordant with imaging findings of disease progression.
